coredata.dc:title: New route to strong, long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence using organic phosphor guest-friendly matrices [Al(DMSO)6]X3 (X=Cl−, Br−)
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhen
Zhang, Jian-Jun
Song, Bo
Fang, Wang-Jian
Zhou, Huajun Andrew
Ni, Jun
Li, Yan-Qin
Liu, Shu-Qin
Duan, Chun-Ying
coredata.dc:description: Developing highly desirable organic compounds with strong long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) under ambient conditions has been a grand challenge. Present here is a new type of crystalline matrices based on ionic solvento complexes, [Al(DMSO)6]X3 (X− = Cl−, Br−) which can trap various trace amount of organic phosphors to prepare low-cost RTP materials. The phosphorescence of such phases in the air at RT can demonstrate both long lifetime (up to 229.7 ms) and high quantum efficiency (up to 15.8%) due to RTP-enhancing X− anions and phosphor-protecting matrices. Further, the guest-friendly feature of the matrices enables the development of a multiple-RTP doped phase via the simultaneous inclusion of two phosphors into one matrix, enabling unprecedented two-dimensional anti-counterfeiting. The work not only finds a new and facile route to develop low-cost RTP materials including multiple-RTP materials but also expands their application domains.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Ionic complex matrix
Doped crystals
Multiple phosphorescence
Two-dimensional anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Matrices being friendly to various organic phosphor guests

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The packing diagram in H2NAIP/1⋅Cl. (b) Luminescence photographs of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl crystals under irradiation and upon removal of a UV lamp (λ = 365 nm).

(a) Steady-state PL (blue, solid line) and corresponding phosphorescence (orange, dash line) spectra of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl and H2NAIP/1⋅Br excited at 365 nm in the air at RT; (b) The histogram of fluorescence and phosphorescence QEs; (c) Time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves of 550 nm emission. Lifetime fit to triple exponential decay (See Table S2). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The concentration ratio ((1,4-NA):H2NAIP) dependent steady-state PL (a) of a series of (H2NAIP+ (1, 4-NA))/1⋅Cl samples (λex = 365 nm). (b) The corresponding CIE coordinates.
Time-dependent phosphorescence spectra of (H2NAIP + (1, 4-NA))/1⋅Cl under 254/365 nm excitation.
Photographs of letters written by (H2NAIP + (1, 4-NA))/1⋅Cl under and after the removal of the UV irradiation (254/365 nm).
(a) Structures of H2NAIP and 1,4-NA. (b) Schematic illustration of the preparation of phosphor/1⋅X crystals.

head.ce:title: New route to strong, long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence using organic phosphor guest-friendly matrices [Al(DMSO)6]X3 (X=Cl−, Br−)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Dalian University of Technology, 116024, Dalian, Liaoning, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Dalian University of Technology, 116024, Dalian, Liaoning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science and Mathematics, Grace College, Winona Lake, 46590, Indiana, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Science and Mathematics, Grace College, Winona Lake, 46590, Indiana, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Zhang Dayu College of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, 116024, Dalian, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Zhang Dayu College of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, 116024, Dalian, Liaoning, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing highly desirable organic compounds with strong long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) under ambient conditions has been a grand challenge. Present here is a new type of crystalline matrices based on ionic solvento complexes, [Al(DMSO)6]X3 (X− = Cl−, Br−) which can trap various trace amount of organic phosphors to prepare low-cost RTP materials. The phosphorescence of such phases in the air at RT can demonstrate both long lifetime (up to 229.7 ms) and high quantum efficiency (up to 15.8%) due to RTP-enhancing X− anions and phosphor-protecting matrices. Further, the guest-friendly feature of the matrices enables the development of a multiple-RTP doped phase via the simultaneous inclusion of two phosphors into one matrix, enabling unprecedented two-dimensional anti-counterfeiting. The work not only finds a new and facile route to develop low-cost RTP materials including multiple-RTP materials but also expands their application domains.

A new type of guest-friendly matrices based on ionic solvento complexes can trap various organic phosphors to prepare low-cost room-temperature phosphorescence materials.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Organic compounds exhibiting long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) have gained significant interest owing to their versatile applications including optoelectronics [1–4], sensing [5–7], bio-imaging [8], and security [9–11]. However, the two conventional kinds of strategies to achieve effective RTP emissions have faced grand challenges. For example, one strategy is to facilitate the intersystem crossing (ISC) process through strong spin-orbit coupling (SOC) from singlet to triplet excited states. Many organic halides have been proven to use their covalently bonded halogen atoms to induce strong SOC via heavy atom effect (HAE), and thus enhance RTP [12–15]. However, their synthetic halogenation reactions involve either complicated synthetic procedures or expensive halogenated reactants. A very recent approach circumvents the complex halogenation reactions and uses halogen counter anions which can also induce RTP via the external HAE to several resultant oxazolium [16], quinolinium [17] and phosphonium [18] halide salts. However, this approach is still limited by the requisite organic cations that are limited in number and difficult to prepare. Besides, none of these compounds have both long lifetime and high quantum efficiency (QE).

The other strategy involves suppressing the non-radiative decay of triplet excitations [19]. Milestones in the development of crystalline-based RTP materials using the strategy include crystallization-induced phosphorescence reported by Tang et al. [20–23], H-aggregation materials developed by Huang and co-workers [24–26]. Other crystalline materials including co-crystals and metal-organic frameworks, etc. can also induce efficient RTP [27–34]. Pioneering works about amorphous systems include polymer-based RTP reported by Fraser and others [35–37], host inclusion enhanced RTP developed by Tian et al. [6,38,39] However, large amounts of organic phosphors with specific molecular configurations are needed, leading to limitations and increased cost.

An alternative method is to use crystalline inorganic salts or organics, amorphous polymers etc. as rigid matrices to capture and immobilize guest phosphors [12,40–50]. Since a small amount of trapped phosphors can generate effective RTP, the cost can be greatly reduced. Kim and co-workers first reported that halogen bonding between host and guest could induce efficient RTP in organic phosphor/organic matrix system [12]. However, detailed research about the system shows it relies on hosts and guests having similar structural entities. Besides, the matrices usually use rigid aromatic compounds modified with specific recognition groups on specific sites, making these structures less flexible and much less guest-friendly in capturing specific phosphors [40–43]. Generally only ionic organic guests can be captured into inorganic salt matrices to form dye inclusion crystals due to the solubility requirements [44,45]. Loading neutral organic guests that are difficult to dissolve in aqueous solutions requires complicated methods [46]. RTP behaviors of most polymer-based doped materials are sensitive to oxygen [37]. A few insensitive examples require critical match of either solubility parameter or formation of multiple hydrogen bonds between the hosts and guests [47–50]. Luminophores can also be deposited on various solid surfaces, combined with micelle or cyclodextrin to induce solid surface RTP [51,52], micelle-stabilized RTP [53] and cyclodextrin-induced RTP respectively [54]. However, these RTP systems generally need special and complicated preparation techniques. Besides, the RTP signals are usually weak and can only be detected by instrument. Generally RTP systems based on carbon dot (CD) luminophores also require highly selective guests that can perfectly match matrices, thus limiting the choices for matrices and guests [55–57]. Therefore, low-cost matrices capable of efficient isolating and rigidifying a broad range of organic phosphors under ambient condition are highly desirable and have been actively pursued.

Herein we employ a new type of guest-friendly matrices based on ionic solvento complexes, i.e., [Al(DMSO)6]X3 (X− = Cl− (1⋅Cl), Br− (1⋅Br)) to trap trace amounts of organic phosphors to develop phosphor/1⋅X doped crystals. Such phases in air can demonstrate RTP with both long lifetime (229.7 ms) and high QE (15.8%) for (5-(1,3-dioxo-1H-benzo[de]isoquinolin-2(3H)-yl)isophthalic acid)/1⋅Cl (H2NAIP/1⋅Cl) doped crystals. The flexible solvento complex cation can adapt its configuration to accommodate various guest molecules in its crystal lattice during crystallization. The X− counter anions can induce effective external HAE and be varied to modulate lifetimes and QEs of RTP. The solvento complexes can uniformly disperse the guest phosphors to prevent aggregation and external species-induced quenching, significantly suppress non-radiative relaxation processes of triplet states. All the advantages indicate the ionic solvento complexes are excellent matrices to prepare effective and cheap RTP materials.

Furthermore, the guest-friendly nature of the matrices makes it feasible to develop a single-phase multiple-RTP material by trapping two phosphors into one such matrix. Compared with the reported preparation methods based on single molecules [58–60], this approach of fabricating single-phase multiple-RTP materials has the characteristics of convenient preparation, easy adjustment of emission wavelength and lifetime. Indeed the resulted double-doped phase exhibits excitation-dependent and time-resolved dual-mode fluorescence/RTP emission, enabling its application in two dimensional (2 D) RTP-based advanced anti-counterfeiting. Therefore, the work can be generalized to not only develop a novel kind of matrix that can induce effective RTP but also extend the applications of tailor-making multiple-RTP materials with desired properties and functionalities.

General. 1,8-Naphthalimide derivative was synthesized according to the literature procedures with some modifications (See Supporting Information 1.1). All other reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The IR spectra were recorded (400-4000 cm−1) from a Nicolet-20DXB spectrometer using KBr pellets. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGAs) were carried out on a TA-Q50 thermogravimetric analyzer under N2 atmosphere with the heating rate of 10 °C/min. Elemental analyses (C, H, N and S) were performed on a Vario EL III elemental analyzer. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) data were collected on D/MAX-2400 X-ray Diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54060 Å) at the scan rate of 5°/min. NMR spectra were recorded on Varian INOVA 400 M and Bruker Avance II 500 M spectrometers with (CD3)2SO as the solvent and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as the internal standard. Electrospray ionization (ESI) mass spectrum of the as-synthesized H2NAIP was recorded on a Q-TOF Micro MS mass spectrometer The UV–visible absorption spectra were collected on a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were recorded on an Olympus FV1000 microscope with a 405 nm laser source as the excitation lamp, and the fluorescence signal was collected at the emission wavelength of 530–630 nm. The steady state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The phosphorescence lifetime, time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) and quantum yields were measured on a FLS920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments) equipped with a microsecond flash-lamp (uF900) and an integrating sphere accessory, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime was measured using a picosecond pulsed diode laser with the excitation wavelength at 375 nm (EPL-375). Photoluminescence graphs were taken using a Canon camera (ixus 230 HS) under UV lamp irradiation.

The [Al(DMSO)6]X3 (X = Cl− (1⋅Cl), Br− (1⋅Br), ClO4 −(1⋅ClO4)) salts and organic phosphors containing RTP doped crystals were prepared by a facile low-temperature evaporation method as described in the following.

3 mL 0.1 M AlCl3 aqueous solution and 5 mL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were placed in a 50 mL beaker and heated at 85 °C for 4 h. The mixture was cooled down to room temperature, affording colorless block crystals. Yield 78% based on Al3+. IR (KBr pellet) cm−1: 3421 (m), 2966 (s), 2899 (w), 1637 (w), 1420 (m), 1059 (s), 1005 (s), 967 (vs), 727 (w), 546 (s). Element anal. Calcd. (%) for 1⋅Cl: C 23.94; H 6.03; N 0.00; S 31.95. Found (%): C 23.87; H 6.12; N 0.00; S 31.91.

Colorless block crystals of 1⋅Br were prepared by following the similar procedure as that for 1⋅Cl, except using AlBr3 instead of AlCl3. Yield 70% based on Al3+. IR (KBr pellet) cm−1: 3419 (m), 2967 (s), 2898 (w), 1638 (w), 1419 (m), 1059 (s), 1003 (s), 963 (vs), 726 (w), 544 (s). Element anal. Calcd. (%) for 1⋅Br: C 19.60; H 4.93; N 0.00; S 26.16. Found (%): C 19.55; H 5.04; N 0.00; S 26.09.

3 mL 0.1 M Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution, 3 mL 0.1 M NaClO4 aqueous solution and 5 mL DMSO were mixed in a 50 mL beaker and heated at 85 °C for 4 h. The mixture was cooled down to room temperature, affording colorless block crystals. Yield: 45% based on Al3+. IR (KBr pellet) cm−1: 3423 (m), 2972 (m), 1643 (w), 1385 (vs), 1145 (m), 1114 (vs), 1087 (vs), 1003 (m), 966 (vs), 631 (m), 544 (m). Element anal. Calcd. (%).for 1⋅ClO4: C 18.15; H 4.57; N 0.00; S 24.23. Found (%): C 18.19; H 4.62; N 0.00; S 24.19.

The phases were prepared by following the similar procedure as that for 1⋅Cl and 1⋅Br with the additional addition of organic molecules in a molar ratio of 2/300 (organics/Al3+). The obtained colorless block crystals were washed three times with small amount of DMSO through vacuum filtration, removed to a dry filtrate paper, wiped with dry filter papers and dried in vacuum.

The phase was prepared by following the similar procedure described above, by simultaneously adding H2NAIP and 1,4-NA into the synthesis system of 1⋅Cl in a molar ratio of 0.3/3/300 (H2NAIP/1,4-NA/Al3+). The obtained colorless block crystals were washed three times with DMSO and dried in vacuum.

Naphthalene derivatives have been extensively explored as phosphors [61]. Herein an initial attempt to develop coordination polymer-featured phosphors by reacting AlCl3 and H2NAIP in DMSO/H2O led to the unexpected formation of colorless block-shaped doped crystals of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl (Scheme 1 ). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses on both single crystals and powders show H2NAIP/1⋅Cl is isostructural to reported 1⋅Cl (Fig. 1 A and S1-3, Table S1) [62], which is further confirmed by their nearly the same IR spectra, elemental and thermogravimetric analysis results (Figure S4–S5). Signals of hydrogen atoms from aromatic rings can be clearly observed in local magnification of 1H NMR spectrum of its DMSO‑d 6 solution (Fig. S6), confirming the presence of trace amount of H2NAIP. Besides, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl features an absorption band in 260–400 nm from H2NAIP due to its n-π* transition (Fig. S7).

In its structure, Al3+ is coordinated by a distorted octahedral {O6} donor set with six O atoms from six monodentate DMSO molecules to afford the solvento [Al(DMSO)6]3+ cation (Scheme 1b, Fig. S1). Each [Al(DMSO)6]3+ is adjacently surrounded by fourteen Cl− while each of the two independent Cl− has six or four neighboring cations respectively. Their electrostatic and C–H⋅⋅⋅Cl interactions lead to a close-packed structure (Scheme 1b, Fig. S2).

Remarkably, H2NAIP/1⋅Cl exhibits ~2.4 s yellow afterglow in the air at RT upon the removal of the UV irradiation of 365 nm (Fig. 1b). Its steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (λex = 365 nm) demonstrates weak emission peaked at ~ 460 nm with lifetime of 8.5 ns (Fig. S8) and strong bands from 525 to 700 nm with major peaks at 550 nm and two shoulder peaks at 595 nm and 650 nm (Fig. 2 a). Its phosphorescence and PL spectra in the 525–700 nm region are very similar, confirming its afterglow behavior. A diluted solution of H2NAIP in DMSO at 77 K (Fig. S9) bears blue emission bands peaked at 452 nm and a similar phosphorescence spectrum peaked at 548, 593 and 649 nm all of which are close to those of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl crystals while 1⋅Cl crystals alone display no PL signals (The photoluminescence QE of pure 1⋅Cl is below 0.01%), suggesting this phase's yellow RTP comes from H2NAIP. In contrast, the as-synthesized H2NAIP crystalline powder only emits a blue emission peaked at 440 nm with a decay time of 10.8 ns and no phosphorescence signal at RT (Fig. S9). Besides, only fluorescence and no phosphorescence peak can be observed when H2NAIP is dispersed into polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) or Zeonex in the air at RT (Fig. S10). All the results clearly suggest that 1⋅Cl is responsible for significantly increasing the RTP lifetime of H2NAIP using its matrix.

Furthermore, the PL spectrum of a physical mixture of H2NAIP and 1⋅Cl with the mole ratio of H2NAIP: 1⋅Cl being 1:50 (Fig. S11) demonstrates only blue fluorescence emission originating from H2NAIP, confirming that this new RTP phase is H2NAIP-doped 1⋅Cl crystals. Laser scanning confocal microscopy measurement indicates that H2NAIP phosphors are evenly distributed throughout the whole 1⋅Cl crystals (Fig. S12).

The ionic complex characteristics of the matrix allow the facile preparation of complexes with different X− counter anions and the investigation of the effects of external X− on the RTP of the doped crystals. H2NAIP/1⋅Br has a similar emission spectrum as that of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl but with weaker fluorescence and stronger phosphorescence peaks (Fig. 2), resulting in an orange-red emission. H2NAIP/1⋅Br and H2NAIP/1⋅Cl have CIE coordinates of (0.44, 0.50) and (0.41, 0.48), respectively. The 550 nm peak in time-resolved emission decay curve of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl gives a lifetime of 228.9 ms. The triple exponential decay of lifetime may be due to heterogeneity of the matrix or long-range energy transfer between proximal phosphors [61].

Remarkably, for the total QE (Ф Total) of 18.7% of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl, the phosphorescence's contribution (Ф P = 15.8%) far exceeds that of the fluorescence (Ф F = 2.9%). In contrast, H2NAIP/1⋅Br shows a much shorter RTP lifetime (15.4 ms) and a higher Ф Total of 21.7% with Ф P up to 17.4% due to the stronger SOC induced by the heavier Br−. Unfortunately, pure phosphor-doped 1⋅I crystals could not be obtained (Fig. S13).

As the concentration of the doping phosphor increases, the I P/I F ratio increases while their emission bands are similar, indicating that the emission color of this fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-emission system can be easily adjusted by tuning the phosphor concentration (Fig. S14). For example, the sample obtained from the starting mole ratio of Al3+:H2NAIP being 300:0.1 has a CIE coordinate very close to that of the pure white-light-emitting (WLE), (0.31, 0.34) vs. (0.33, 0.33).

Another approach to obtain RTP doped crystals is to directly immerse 1⋅Cl crystals into a H2NAIP/DMSO solution at 60 °C for several hours. The phase with an immersion time of 6 h, namely H2NAIP/1⋅Cl-1, exhibits similar emission bands but with an I P/I F intensity ratio lower than that of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl (Fig. S15). Although its phosphorescence lifetime (227.9 ms) and Ф F (3.0%) are close to those in H2NAIP/1⋅Cl, it has a much lower Ф P (0.6%) possibly due to fewer phosphors that can be embedded into the matrix via this alternative approach. Besides, its trapped phosphors may be mainly located in the region near the crystal surfaces and the effect of immobilizing phosphor molecules may not be as strong as that in H2NAIP/1⋅Cl (Scheme S1). Physical mixing experimental results (Fig. S11) show that RTP cannot be achieved when H2NAIP is only adsorbed on the surface of 1⋅Cl, while investigations of different soaking time reveal the amount of trapped H2NAIP increases as the soaking time increases. Both results indicate the formation of this RTP phase may involve a process of matrix dissolution, the capturing H2NAIP by the dissolution-induced defective caves, the recrystallization of 1⋅Cl and the accompanied covering of the phosphor (Scheme S1).

The doped crystals are soluble in H2O, slightly soluble in DMSO, insoluble in other common organic solvents. To study the effect of different solvents on their RTP properties, the phosphorescence lifetimes of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl samples wetted by 12 different organic solvent are investigated (Fig. S16). The results show the phosphorescence lifetimes of all the wetted samples are almost unchanged. H2NAIP/1⋅Cl can displays RTP for less than 3 h when being exposed to the air at RT due to its hygroscopicity. However, the hygroscopicity effect can be overcome or minimized when the crystals are doped into PMMA film (Fig. S17). Besides, H2NAIP/1⋅Cl is stable for at least half a year when being stored in desiccator (Fig. S18). All the results prove the excellent protection performance of the complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Matrices being friendly to various organic phosphor guests
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the capability of the matrices to adapt to different organic phosphor guests, we investigated other organic molecules with different sizes and functional groups. The strong dissolving ability of DMSO enables these molecules to be easily captured into 1⋅X (X = Cl, Br) matrices to form doped phases via the same synthetic approach. All the doped phases exhibit RTP behaviors (Table S2-S3), proving that 1⋅X (X = Cl, Br) as host matrices can efficiently promote RTP of a broad range of organic phosphors. The coordination configuration of [Al(DMSO)6]3+ is believed to be able to accommodate different phosphors to form these doped crystals during crystallization. Its flexibility is attributed to the hexacoordinate metal ions and DMSO ligands (especially monodentate-coordinated) both of which can distort their coordination configurations to accommodate different coordination environments. Among all these doped crystals, (3-biphenylcarboxylic acid)/1⋅Cl and H2NAIP/1⋅Br show the longest phosphorescence lifetime of 1811.7 ms and the highest phosphorescence QE of 17.4%, respectively. More in-depth investigation about the correlation between the phosphors’ configurations and the luminescence performances of the doped phases is ongoing.

To get more information about the host-guest interactions inside the caves, computational simulations based on the non-covalent interaction (NCI) methodology were performed on H2NAIP and 1, 4-NA (1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid) using independent gradient model (IGM) [63]. Depending on the sizes of the guest phosphors, one to several [Al(DMSO)6]3+ cations were removed from the matrix to form defective caves to capture them. The configurations of the guests inside the caves were optimized and the related host-guest interactions were calculated (Fig. S19-S25). The results show the guests can be well stabilized by the abundant weak interactions including C–H (aromatic)⋅⋅⋅X−, COOH⋅⋅⋅X−, CO⋅⋅⋅CH3 and COOH⋅⋅⋅CH3. Thus, like other crystalline matrices, the host matrix can uniformly disperse the phosphors to effectively prevent the quenching induced by aggregation and external species, e.g., oxygen or moisture. Besides, the close packing of matrices and the abundant intermolecular interactions between the phosphor and matrices may also be helpful in stabilizing triplet energy by reducing molecular motion.

To determine whether the HAE of halogen counter-anion or the rigid environment provided by the matrix plays a decisive role in the RTP behaviours of the doped crystals, we synthesized 1⋅ClO4 and H2NAIP/1⋅ClO4 and studies their luminescent properties in the air at RT. Fig. S27 shows that only fluorescence but no phosphorescence peak can be observed, indicating that the external HAE of the halogen counter anion instead of the rigid environment plays the decisive role. Neither S in DMSO nor Cl in ClO4 − contributes to the HAE, possibly due to the longer Cl (S)⋅⋅⋅C (aromatic) separations induced by the steric hindrance of C (DMSO) or O (ClO4 −) atoms. In contrast, rigid environment is usually believed to be the main factor in inducing RTP behavior of many doped systems, such as organic phosphor/inorganic matrix, CDs/aluminum sulfate and organic phosphor/polymer matrix [37,44,45,55–57].

Herein we developed a more effective and convenient approach to introduce X− counter anions simply by using different aluminum halide starting materials and achieve both long lifetime and high QEs simultaneously. This approach is much more convenient than the previously reported introduction of covalently bonded X into matrix molecules [12,41]. Computational simulation results show the shortest intermolecular Cl⋅⋅⋅C (aromatic) separations in H2NAIP/1⋅Cl and (1, 4-NA)/1⋅Cl are 3.32 Å and 3.55 Å (Fig. S26) respectively, which are short enough to promote efficient ISC through external HAE [64]. Besides, 1⋅X series are isostructural and provide an excellent platform to study the effects of different X−. For example, the emission colors can also be adjusted via changing counter anions or their concentrations (Figs. S14 and S28). Compared to 1⋅Br, 1⋅Cl induces phosphors with longer lifetimes but lower QEs due to the different heavy atom enhancement effect.

Both steady-state and phosphorescence spectra of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl under 254 nm and 365 nm excitation (Figs. S30 and S31) are almost the same except that the former has a much weaker phosphorescent peak intensity and shorter lifetime (221.8 ms). In contrast, (1, 4-NA)/1⋅Cl only shows one strong fluorescent peak at 410 nm (λex = 365 nm) or 403 nm (λex = 254 nm) in steady-state spectra. Its phosphorescence spectrum has no signal under 365 nm excitation, but bears a series of emission peaks at 507, 545 and 588 nm under 254 nm excitation with the lifetime of 507 nm peak being 519.3 ms.

However, for the double-doped phase, the lifetime of the yellow emission from H2NAIP under 365 nm excitation is only slightly reduced (τP = 227.6 ms monitored at 550 nm, Fig. S32 ) while the lifetime of the green emission from 1, 4-NA under 254 nm excitation is much reduced and reaches 493.9 ms (monitored at 507 nm). The reason could be due to the resonance energy transfer between the two phosphors, as revealed by the different degrees of spectral overlap between the absorption of H2NAIP/1⋅Cl and the emission from (1, 4-NA)/1⋅Cl excited by the two different wavelengths (Fig. S33).

Obviously, this method of obtaining single-phase multiple-RTP materials is much more effective and convenient than the traditional multilayer deposition method [65,66]. The preparation of such dopants containing three or more types of phosphors and having more RTP emission is ongoing.

Such a unique multi-emissive material can be further used as an “ink” to fabricate an excitation- and time-dependent PL anti-counterfeiting system. In a prove-of-concept experiment, letters “DUT” were written on a piece of non-luminescent paper and emit blue emission under 254 nm irradiation (Fig. 5 ). When the excitation is turned off, all letters immediately exhibit yellowish green phosphorescence emission which lasts up to ~5 s. Under 365 nm irradiation, the letters exhibit a bright white color. When the excitation is turned off, they display deep yellow afterglow up to 2.4 s. The sensitive dependence of the emission colors on excitation and time can be attributed to the combined effects from the two included phosphors. Among the total of four colors, i.e., blue, white, yellowish green, and yellow, two are gradually weakened over time and the change can be clearly detected by naked eyes. Such 2D RTP-based anti-counterfeiting is made possible by the guest-friendly matrix that can host two phosphors simultaneously. In contrast, traditional 1D RTP-based anti-counterfeiting relies on materials with sole phosphors [67–69]. The above results also suggest that it is necessary for the two captured phosphors to have significantly different RTP lifetimes, excitation and emission wavelengths in order for the double-doped system to have such 2D anti-counterfeiting behaviors.

In summary, we successfully developed a facile and new approach to prepare a new type of low-cost room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with both long lifetime and high phosphorescence efficiency in the air at room temperature. The materials are composed of trace amount of trapped organic phosphors, crystalline complex matrices based on [Al(DMSO)6]3+ and halogen counter anions. The matrices is not picky for guest phosphors, owning to the excellent solubility of DMSO solvent and the flexible [Al(DMSO)6]3+ cations. The halogen counter anions can be introduced with ease to facilitate intersystem crossing for high phosphorescence efficiency via external heavy atom effect and be tuned to study the effects of different halogen ions. The resultant close packing crystalline matrices can effectively suppress the nonradiative relaxation of triplet excitations and protect phosphors from being quenched by external species. Furthermore, the photoluminescence colors of the doped crystals can be effectively adjusted through controlling the phosphor concentration, counter anions, and doping of different and multiple phosphors. All these advantages make it feasible to develop a low-cost multiple-RTP material and finds applications in two-dimensional RTP-based anti-counterfeiting. The work not only provides a unique perspective to develop low-cost and effective RTP materials based on the solvento complex-type matrix but also highlight a new strategy to achieve two-dimensional anti-counterfeiting materials.

Zhen Liu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jian-Jun Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Bo Song: Resources. Wang-Jian Fang: Investigation. Huajun Andrew Zhou: Writing - review & editing. Jun Ni: Data curation. Yan-Qin Li: Visualization. Shu-Qin Liu: Visualization. Chun-Ying Duan: Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This material is based upon work supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21871038, 21471024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108323.
